title: Sinh-Gordon equation and application to the geometry of CMC surfaces.
speaker: Laurent Hauswirth
tags: Workshop on Mean Curvature and Regularity

proof_stages.label: Sinh-Gordon equations as Gauss-Codazzi and harmonic-map data
proof_stages.summary: The talk identifies a sinh-Gordon-type equation with holomorphic Hopf differential as the local integrability condition for CMC surfaces, minimal surfaces in product spaces, and harmonic maps into constant-curvature targets.

proof_stages.label: Normalization of the Hopf differential and annulus moduli
proof_stages.summary: Assuming the Hopf differential has no zeros, a conformal coordinate is chosen so that it is normalized to one quarter, and the central moduli problem is formulated for sinh-Gordon solutions and the associated minimal or CMC annuli with periods.

proof_stages.label: Linearized sinh-Gordon equation and hierarchy of infinitesimal flows
proof_stages.summary: Tangent vectors to the moduli space satisfy the linearized sinh-Gordon equation, and a Bäcklund-type recursion generates an infinite hierarchy of such linearized solutions whose integrability as genuine deformations becomes the next problem.

proof_stages.label: Geometric conversion to Jacobi fields with horizontal correction
proof_stages.summary: Normal Jacobi fields on the corresponding surface are related to linearized sinh-Gordon fields after adding horizontal correction terms that keep the Hopf differential and preferred coordinate fixed.

proof_stages.label: Schiffman curvature field and circle-foliation consequences
proof_stages.summary: The derivative of the curvature of planar section curves is shown to be a Jacobi field matching a higher member of the hierarchy, yielding consequences for annuli foliated by circles and for index-based rigidity.

proof_stages.label: Finite type, spectral curve, and global integrable flows
proof_stages.summary: Finite type is introduced as eventual linear dependence in the hierarchy, giving a spectral curve whose genus organizes known families and whose integrable flows exist for all time while preserving periods and energy levels.

proof_stages.label: Period problems, bifurcation, and embeddedness applications
proof_stages.summary: The finite-type deformation theory is applied to period-closing and bifurcation questions, leading to uniqueness and Alexandrov-embeddedness results and explaining how the length of a preserved period controls possible bifurcations.

proof_stages.label: Extensions to negative curvature, Heisenberg geometry, and Dirac potentials
proof_stages.summary: The same sinh-Gordon framework is extended to CMC surfaces in Minkowski space, minimal surfaces in Heisenberg and related product geometries, and a spinor/Dirac formulation leading to energy-minimization questions.

examples.name: Minimal annuli between parallel planes
examples.note: Minimal annuli bounded by curves in parallel planes illustrate why a nonzero Hopf differential can be normalized so that the third coordinate supplies a useful conformal parameter and period problem.
examples.engagement_reason: he applies the Hopf-differential normalization Q=1/4 and third-coordinate parametrization to minimal annuli between parallel planes, relating it to the annulus period problem

passes.reason: Ordinary minimal annuli between parallel planes are used to illustrate the Hopf-differential normalization and period setup.

ordinary minimal annuli are used as the basic period-problem setting rather than as a special or refuting case
Minimal annuli are area-critical surfaces used here to illustrate the third-coordinate parametrization.

ordinary minimal annuli between parallel planes are used as the motivating period-problem class, not as a failure case
used as a standard class where the Hopf differential can be normalized and the annulus period problem studied

used as a standard annulus/period-problem setting, with no special intrinsic extremality or disproof role
Used as a standard minimal-annulus setting where the Hopf differential can be normalized by the height coordinate.

examples.name: Derivative fields omega_x and omega_y
examples.note: The coordinate derivatives of a sinh-Gordon solution are used as concrete first solutions of the linearized equation and then reinterpreted geometrically as elementary Jacobi/reparametrization fields.
examples.engagement_reason: he differentiates the sinh-Gordon equation to get Lωx=Lωy=0, complexifies to ωz, applies the Bäcklund hierarchy, and computes the associated correction fields

passes.reason: The fields omega_x and omega_y are the trivial coordinate-derivative/base Jacobi fields in the linearized hierarchy.
the derivative fields are the trivial translation/reparametrization kernel fields of the linearized equation
the derivative Jacobi fields are the trivial linearized directions coming from differentiating/reparametrizing the solution
The fields omega_x and omega_y are the trivial derivative/Jacobi modes coming from differentiating the equation and reparametrizing.
The fields omega_x and omega_y are the trivial infinitesimal fields coming from differentiating/reparametrizing the solution.
The fields omega_x and omega_y are the trivial/base Jacobi fields coming from differentiating the equation and reparametrization or ambient motions.
omega_x and omega_y are trivial symmetry/reparametrization Jacobi modes obtained by differentiating the equation
omega_x and omega_y are the trivial derivative/Killing-type first Jacobi fields in the linearized hierarchy
these are the trivial symmetry/reparametrization Jacobi fields obtained by differentiating the sinh-Gordon solution
The fields are the trivial first linearized solutions obtained by differentiating the equation, with one giving only reparametrization.
the coordinate-derivative Jacobi fields are the trivial symmetry/reparametrization directions of the linearized equation
They are universal trivial linearized fields from differentiating the equation, including reparametrization/Killing directions.

examples.name: Circle-bounded annuli and Riemann examples
examples.note: Annuli bounded by two round circles and the classical Riemann examples illustrate the consequence that vanishing of the Schiffman field forces foliation by circles, with index and nodal-domain arguments giving geometric rigidity.
examples.engagement_reason: he constructs the Schiffman Jacobi field from curvature derivatives, relates its vanishing to circle foliations, and uses nodal-domain/index reasoning for circle-bounded annuli and Riemann examples

passes.reason: Round-boundary and circularly foliated annuli are highly regular, and the positive-curvature circle-bound examples are invoked to rule out a uniform index bound.
round-circle boundary and circle foliation make the Riemann annuli highly regular model cases
round-circle/Riemann annuli are singled out by their circle foliation and regular geometry, not used to disprove a claim
The annuli are minimal/CMC critical surfaces with the extra regularity of round-circle boundary or circular foliation, used as model cases rather than refutations.
Round-boundary annuli and Riemann examples are special circle-foliated regular surfaces, not sporadic or adversarial here.
Round-boundary/circularly foliated annuli are highly regular, and the positive-curvature circle examples are invoked to defeat a hoped-for uniform index bound.

round-boundary or circle-foliated Riemann annuli are highly regular symmetric model cases in the Schiffman/index discussion
Riemann and circle-foliated annuli are invoked as highly regular circle-foliated model cases rather than as refutations
Round-circle boundary and circle foliations make these annuli/Riemann examples highly regular model cases rather than failures of a claim.
round-boundary and circle-foliated Riemann-type annuli are highly regular model cases rather than counterexamples
Round-circle and Riemann annuli serve as highly regular circle-foliated models in the Schiffman/index discussion.

examples.name: Low spectral-genus model families
examples.note: Flat planes, cylinders, Clifford tori, unduloids, nodoids, catenoids, helicoids, and Riemann-type families make the abstract spectral-genus stratification concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: he lists the genus 0, 1, and 2 spectral-family models and their known geometric representatives without deriving or computing them

passes.reason: The spectral-genus 0, 1, and 2 model families sit at the lowest-complexity end, with the genus-zero zero-potential base case and rotational or circularly foliated symmetry.
spectral genera 0–2 are bottom finite-type cases, including flat, rotational, and circle-foliated model surfaces
the genus 0/1/2 families lie at the small-genus boundary of the finite-type hierarchy, with genus 0 the omega=0 base case

examples.name: Heat equation contrast
examples.note: The heat equation is invoked only as a contrast: unlike heat flow, the integrable deformation flow preserves an energy level and therefore does not converge to the round or genus-one models.
examples.engagement_reason: he only contrasts the integrable flow with the heat equation by saying heat flow decreases energy

passes.reason: The heat equation is cited only as the standard dissipative contrast to the energy-preserving integrable flow.

examples.name: Two-covered unstable catenoid bifurcation
examples.note: The free-boundary annulus problem with a twofold cover of an unstable catenoid illustrates how period and boundary constraints can allow bifurcation to a non-circle-foliated annulus while the stable catenoid does not bifurcate.
examples.engagement_reason: he sets up the circle-boundary bifurcation problem and traces how the two-covered unstable catenoid bifurcates while the stable one does not

passes.reason: The rotational catenoid is a symmetric critical bifurcation point whose double cover produces non-circular annuli with the same covered circle boundary.
a nonprimitive double cover of the rotational unstable catenoid is a critical bifurcation point producing non-circle-foliated annuli
the rotational two-covered unstable catenoid is a non-primitive bifurcation saddle producing fixed-boundary non-circle-foliated annuli

The doubly covered unstable catenoid is a multiply covered rotational critical surface at a bifurcation point producing non-circle-foliated annuli, so it refutes uniqueness or circular-foliation expectations.
The rotational two-covered unstable catenoid bifurcates to non-circle-foliated annuli, defeating naive uniqueness or foliation expectations.
The rotationally symmetric unstable catenoid at a two-cover bifurcation point gives non-circular annuli, obstructing uniqueness or circular-foliation expectations for covered circle boundaries.
the double cover of the unstable catenoid is used to show boundary-fixed uniqueness or circle-foliation can fail, and it is a rotational critical bifurcation case
the double-covered rotational unstable catenoid is a critical non-primitive bifurcation point producing fixed-boundary non-circle-foliated annuli, so uniqueness or foliation fails
the double-covered unstable catenoid is a rotationally symmetric bifurcation point showing fixed-boundary uniqueness or circle-foliation can fail
The two-cover of the unstable catenoid is a critical bifurcation case producing non-circle-foliated annuli with the same covered circular boundary.
the rotational unstable catenoid at a double-cover bifurcation shows fixed circular-boundary uniqueness or circle-foliation can fail
The doubled unstable catenoid is a rotational critical bifurcation point producing non-circle-foliated fixed-boundary annuli, so uniqueness or foliation fails.

examples.name: Clifford torus and Villarceau-period comparison
examples.note: The Clifford torus and a longer Villarceau-circle period are used to show why the available closed period controls whether genus-two bifurcations can occur while preserving Alexandrov embeddedness.
examples.engagement_reason: he compares the Clifford torus periods with Villarceau-circle periods and reasons through which period choices allow genus-two bifurcations and Alexandrov embedded annuli

passes.reason: The Clifford torus is the homogeneous omega=0 spectral-genus-zero torus, and the longer Villarceau period gives bifurcations that obstruct a naive period-only embeddedness argument.
the homogeneous Clifford torus is a shortest-period extremal model, while the Villarceau-period construction loses Alexandrov embeddedness
the highly symmetric Clifford torus is an extremal short-period model, while the Villarceau-period branch shows closed-period bifurcation need not preserve Alexandrov embeddedness
The Clifford torus is a highly symmetric minimal/Willmore-extremal torus used to compare short embedded periods with longer Villarceau periods.
The highly symmetric Clifford torus has the shortest relevant period, while the longer Villarceau period gives a genus-two bifurcation showing period-independent no-bifurcation fails.
The highly symmetric Clifford torus is an extremal short-period model, while the Villarceau-period bifurcation shows that closing a longer period need not preserve Alexandrov embeddedness.
the Villarceau-period branch is an enemy case showing a longer closed period can bifurcate but lose Alexandrov embeddedness, while the Clifford torus is symmetric and extremal
the homogeneous omega=0 Clifford torus is a lowest-genus shortest-period case, and the longer Villarceau period shows closed-period bifurcation need not remain Alexandrov embedded
the highly symmetric Clifford torus and longer Villarceau period illustrate the period obstruction and the loss of Alexandrov embeddedness under naive bifurcation
The highly symmetric Clifford torus is used with a longer Villarceau period to show genus-two bifurcation can occur if one drops the shortest-period/Alexandrov-embedded constraint.
the Clifford torus is a maximally regular homogeneous torus and extremal base case used to illustrate shortest versus Villarceau periods
The homogeneous spectral-genus-zero Clifford torus has the shortest-period obstruction, while a longer Villarceau period gives bifurcations that lose Alexandrov embeddedness.

examples.name: Horizontal annuli in Nil_3 with exponential Gauss map
examples.note: The constructed Nil_3 annuli with a Gauss map of exponential form illustrate how the sinh-Gordon/harmonic-map framework extends to Heisenberg geometry and can produce properly embedded surfaces with little visible symmetry.
examples.engagement_reason: he gives an explicit exponential-form Gauss map in Nil_3 with its differential condition and says how it integrates to horizontal annuli without symmetry

passes.reason: The explicit Nil_3 horizontal annuli with exponential Gauss map are presented as examples with no ambient symmetry, defeating a rotational-symmetry expectation.
the Nil_3 annuli are exhibited to show that horizontal annuli can occur with no ambient symmetry
the Nil_3 annuli with exponential Gauss map are presented as properly embedded annuli with no surface symmetry, defeating a symmetry expectation

the Nil_3 annuli with exponential Gauss map are constructed to have no surface symmetry, defeating a symmetry-only expectation
the Nil_3 horizontal annuli are exhibited to show that such annuli can exist without any surface symmetry
